sources.short: Funeral Oration on Judge Eben Newton by Dr. Wm Dickson, Nov 1885

open_questions.question: Is Isaac Newton Jr. the son of HD0154 (Capt. Isaac Newton b.1748)?
open_questions.notes: The funeral oration says Eben Newton's grandfather was 'a large landholder of Goshen with three sons: Enoch, Samuel, and Isaac.' H00148 (John Newton b.1726, Hooker descendant gen 5) had documented sons Samuel (HD0156) and Isaac (HD0154) — two of the three. H00148's research notes say he 'purchased many acres of wild land with son Isaac.' If HD0154 (Capt. Isaac Newton b.1748) is the Goshen landholder grandfather and Isaac Newton Jr. is his son, then Isaac Jr., John Newton (X01993), and Judge Eben Newton are all 6th/7th-generation Hooker descendants through H00148 → HD0007 → H00044 → H00012 → H00003 (Mary Hooker Newton) → Thomas Hooker. This would make HD2915-HD2924 double Hooker descendants through both the Thompson/Cowles line and the Newton/Hooker line. Needs primary source confirmation — check Goshen CT vital records and Newton genealogy.
open_questions.action: Check Goshen CT vital records; verify HD0154 children; consult Newton genealogy

cross_connections.display_label: Another Isaac Newton, same generation, born 4 years apart — probable cousins

narrative_blocks.header: Invalid fourteen years, one side paralyzed
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Newton spent the last fourteen years of his life as an invalid, with one side paralyzed and 'almost helpless.' The condition is consistent with either a stroke around 1798 or progressive degenerative disease in his fifties. He died in poverty in 1812 at Goshen Connecticut at age sixty-seven, his wife Rebecca Minott saving only fifty acres at his death — and even those were sold by the town selectmen in 1816-1817 for ten dollars an acre.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited father's property, lost it, died poor
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac came into possession of all his father Samuel Newton's property and lost it before his death — a complete inheritance-to-poverty reversal in a single lifetime. He fathered ten children with Rebecca Minott (married Jan 10, 1769) before his fourteen-year invalidism set in. The two-Isaac-Newton family network in central Connecticut — Isaac of Woodbridge born 1744 and Isaac of Durham born 1740, both Hooker descendants through Samuel and Mary Hooker Newton — were almost certainly known to each other.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Woodbridge 1744, baptized Parish of Amity
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Newton was born September 2, 1744 in Woodbridge Connecticut and was baptized the same day at the Parish of Amity. He descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker in the fifth generation through Mary Hooker Newton, his great-great-grandmother, by way of the Woodbridge Newton branch.

INST0045.name: Connecticut Land Company
INST0045.notes: Formed 1795 by 36 Connecticut investors who purchased the entire Connecticut Western Reserve from the State of Connecticut for over $1,000,000 — land the state had 'reserved' for citizens whose property was damaged by the British in the Revolution. In April 1807, Richfield Township was acquired for $26,087 by seven men: Col. Benjamin Tallmadge (H00757), Solomon Lathrop, Jeremiah Wilcox, Uriel Holmes Jr., Pierpont Edwards (H00386), Daniel Green, and Jonathan Dwight Sr. — two of the seven being Hooker descendants. Pierpont Edwards (H00386) invested $60,000 — approximately 5% of total company holdings. His son John Stark Edwards (HD2044) served as his first agent in the Reserve, but died Feb 22, 1813 in Warren, Trumbull County OH age 35 — the year Whittlesey rode through the black swamp and nearly died at Fort Meigs. Pierpont Township, Ashtabula County, Ohio is named for Edwards. In 1811, Heman Oviatt (husband of HD2934 Eunice Newton Oviatt) bought the northwest quarter of Richfield Township from Col. Tallmadge for $1.25 an acre. John Newton (HD2925) arrived in Richfield in the early 1820s and purchased most of the east center land, building the home later owned by Homer Carter. Elisha Whittlesey (X01997) arrived in Canfield June 27, 1806 — the same summer the company was active — and became public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve. The company was ultimately a financial failure. (Richfield Township History; Ohio History Journal; Whittlesey Genealogy #310)
